draft.introduction: The Melian Dialogue (Thucydides 5.84–116) records the confrontation between Athens and the neutral island of Melos in 416 BCE. The Athenians argue that “the strong do what they can and the weak suffer what they must.” It is the starkest articulation of power politics in Western literature.

translation.text: Athenians: For ourselves, we shall not trouble you with specious pretences, and we hope that you will not think to influence us by claiming that you did not join the Lacedaemonians. You know as well as we do that right, as the world goes, is only in question between equals in power, while the strong do what they can and the weak suffer what they must.
translation.original_text: ὑμεῖς δὲ τοίνυν οὔτε αὐτοὶ μετ' ὀνομάτων καλῶν

translation.text: Melians: But we trust that the gods will give us fortune as good as yours, because we stand blameless against those who are not just. Athenians: Our opinion of the gods and our knowledge of men lead us to conclude that it is a general and necessary law of nature to rule wherever one can.
translation.original_text: τὴν μὲν τύχην ἐκ τοῦ θείου ἐλπίζομεν

translator_notes.note: The Athenian thesis—that justice operates only between equals—is the purest expression of might-makes-right in the Western canon. Thucydides presents it without explicit moral commentary, but the narrative that follows (Athens’s Sicilian disaster) serves as implicit judgment. The dialogue became a foundational text for international relations theory.
